summary: This is a mock interview for an ML Engineer role. The host, Alexi, introduces the interviewer, Dennis, and the candidate, Mahilo. Dennis outlines the interview structure (experience, hard skills, coding task). Mahilo discusses his experience, primarily in generative AI and GCP, then answers technical questions on ML concepts like data sampling, labeling (hand vs. natural), handling missing values, bias-variance tradeoff, ensemble learning, batch vs. online prediction, cloud vs. edge deployment, model size reduction techniques, data distribution shift, MLOps environment standardization, prompt engineering, RAG systems, and fine-tuning LLMs. The interview concludes with a brief coding task explanation and feedback from Dennis.
quality_flags: Employee's English has some grammatical imperfections and word choice issues, but the meaning is generally clear. Corrections were made lightly without altering meaning.
Interviewer sometimes interrupts or clarifies, which is natural in an interview. Turns are segmented to reflect distinct questions or clarifications.
The coding task explanation is cut short, as the interviewer asks the employee to explain his approach verbally.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Nice to meet you. I suggest we split our interview into three parts. The first part will be about your experience, what projects you worked on, and what achievements you are most proud of. The second part will be about your hard skills, so I will ask different technical questions to understand your knowledge and understanding of ML principles. The third part will be a coding task where I will check your Python knowledge to see if you are able to solve real problems. Got it.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: As I said, let's start with the first section. Could you tell more about your experience, what projects you worked on, and what your achievements are?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, Dennis. Thank you. Nice to meet you. About me: most of the time I'm studying, but during those studying steps, especially like certification preparations, I gain some practice step by step. Especially in the last two years, I worked mostly with GCP Cloud, specifically with generative AI capabilities. I was working in a company that is an integrator on the market, working with different vendors. I was a pre-sale engineer for GCP Cloud, focusing on generative AI. My tasks were mostly to talk to clients to understand what they wanted to achieve with this new technology. Most of the time, I worked on PoCs, proof-of-concept for business ideas, to understand if they were possible to implement. It wasn't a lot of production coding, but for the last half-year, I've been working on an internal product, also with generative artificial intelligence. My task is to adopt, create, and adapt an SQL agent, which is a different approach to picking up knowledge. Most generative applications with private domain data work by semantic search. For me, the approach with structured data is interesting: how we can work with it, especially how we can combine it with semantic search. So, at the moment, I'm approaching the agentic flow in generative applications to combine these two great capabilities. I'm also exploring a SQL generation agent that can talk to databases or local files with structured data. So, that is my main task and main field of work at the moment.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, got it. I wanted to ask about your experience with GCP. What services did you work with? What did they actually do for your clients and for your solutions?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, actually, there's an interesting tool called Agent Builder, which helps build agentic applications out-of-the-box with great capabilities. Before that, to understand how it works, I was learning Dialogflow, which is also a framework to build connections between a person's intent and the program's fallback. So, understanding this routine and intents was my way to understand how everything works in NLP from that side. But also, from this out-of-the-box tool, I started to use code to customize tasks, and with code, I became familiar with LangChain, which is a great framework to build these applications. Regarding the tasks I've done, one was searching by image in an image database. This was an interesting business task from a client: if someone took a photo of something, was it possible to find this item in the store? I used embeddings. Specifically, I used different costs. First, I created descriptions of the pictures, then stored the descriptions in a vector database. Next, a new incoming image was again decoded into a natural description, and according to semantic search, it picked up similar images from the vector database. I also built a chatbot for one of our customers and connected it to Telegram. That was a new experience for me, using a new kind of UI to talk to an agent or chatbot. Interesting tasks also included one client who had their call center calls recorded. My task was to extract text from those audio calls to understand if the sentiment was positive, negative, or neutral. Another interesting task was for a customer who had many typical old documents. Their task was to recognize entities on those documents. So, I recognized the documents, and as output, the LLM gave me a JSON structured answer with all the entities I needed from those documents. With the help of LLM, it was really possible to achieve good results, especially with large amounts of data. So, it was different tasks in different fields.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, so as I understood, most of your experience is about generative AI. You didn't work with traditional ML, right? For example, computer vision or working with structured data?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I worked with those kinds of tasks mostly during training. I'm preparing for ML engineering for GCP and also for ML engineer for Azure. There are a lot of tasks, especially on playbooks, where I had the opportunity to get some experience with traditional ML, and I also picked up a lot of theory from there. Yes, but directly, I actually have a lack of experience with production-grade ML systems.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, got it. Sounds good. I think we are ready to move to the second part. Let's begin with an example. Let's assume we have a product, and we've already gathered the business requirements, translated them into ML objectives, and data engineers have helped us collect the required data. Now, your task as an ML engineer is to sample from this data and label it for actual ML model training. My first question is: what sampling methods to get training data do you know?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So we have raw data, right? And we need to have some samples from the data for our next steps, correct?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Not necessarily raw data. This data can already be cleaned by data engineers. But for example, there's a huge amount of data, and it might be expensive to train the model on all of it. You want to get a sample of it and train your model on this sample. What are the strategies to sample this data so that model training is efficient and the model will perform as expected?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, I understand. So there is sampling from the data. I know one approach, maybe the simplest one, is randomized sampling, where all of our data has an equal opportunity to be picked up for our sample. It's a statistical method. Also, there could be options like a kind of random but more systematic sampling, where we have some groups and pick up an element from that group, and then with some step, we pick up elements from those subgroups. It's important to know that we have all important data for our sample, and in that case, stratified sampling is common when we have subgroups, especially when some subgroups are represented with a low amount of examples. It could be an option to pick up all important information from there. Also, there could be convenient sampling, just picking up all available data that we can use at the moment without any specific approach.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: What is the main drawback of this approach? If you sample the data just by convenience, what is the main drawback?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: It could be fluid data, not important data, or data that doesn't have good quality to train our model. It could be not representative. So, we are not sure about the quality of our sample in that case.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, I would say the main disadvantage you mentioned is that the data can be not representative. So it doesn't represent the population from which these instances are sampled. I understand what you mean. Okay. And you also mentioned simple random sampling. But what is its main drawback, and how does it compare to stratified sampling?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: In simple random sampling, there's a possibility that we don't pick up some examples or entities that are important, have good quality, or could be important for the representation of our data, because we do it just randomly. In the case of stratified sampling, we have groups that are represented, not like their population, but some specific probability on that group. When we use stratified sampling, we can be sure that all groups are represented in our sample. So, when our model is learning, we know that important examples are represented there, and we've covered all our options, the spread of that data.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. I think it makes sense. Let's then talk about the data labeling process. Do you know the difference between hand labels and natural labels?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yes. Hand labels, I think, are probably the main approach, especially historically, when a human takes an example and assigns a label to that example. In natural labels, it's a different approach where we have some data that is like an added bonus to our data; it already has some information. For example, it could be a timestamp in the data, or it could be part of the file name that could be used by the model. Or, in that kind of labeling, it could be used to assign some simple, maybe pre-labeled information, like to pick up some information to categorize it for the moment. So, it's some additional information that comes to us with our examples, correct?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: I would say you've clearly described hand labeling. It's obvious that people label the data manually. But regarding natural labels, I think you were going in the wrong direction. I'll provide an example. Let's assume there's a recommendation system that recommends different movies or products that users will mostly like. We can measure the effectiveness of the recommendation system by, for example, whether the user clicks on this product or movie or not. If the user clicks on this product, we log this information, and we obtain the natural label that this recommendation is good enough to be clicked. Then we can reuse this data for model training. So, we receive feedback from the system on whether users click on these products or not, and then we can reuse this data for retraining. This is the nature of natural labels: they are derived automatically by the system. So, it is kind of like additional information that we can achieve from our systems, right?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: It's not like additional information; it's just by nature. We show our products. Let's assume we're building a recommendation system, and as a first iteration, it's a simple rule-based system. We recommend products based on popularity, and that's it. We don't consider user interests and so on. When we show these products, users either click on them or not. If the user clicks on a product, we understand that the user likes this product, and we can use it as a label. This can be reused for further model training, and then we can replace the simple rule-based system with an ML approach.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay. I didn't work with them directly, but I was thinking that maybe it's some algorithms picking up that information from the source of data. Okay, maybe here I'm a bit wrong.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. So, let's talk about the problem of lacking labels in our training data. Let's assume we have data, but we don't have enough labels for model training. Do you know some methods to overcome this issue to prepare a labeled dataset for further model training, except hand labeling, because it might be expensive and slow? What other methods do you know?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Well, I know it's also possible to use some machine learning algorithms to create new labels, especially like unsupervised learning or self-supervised learning, where these algorithms can create new labels according to a limited amount of available labels. This is in the case of semi-supervised learning. In unsupervised learning, I think it's also used for data preparation for classification tasks when, by some attributes or data information, it can prepare or do some pre-classification based on their attributes. So, with those algorithms, we can create some kind of labeled data which might not have sufficient quality, but it will be useful for next steps, like to prepare good, high-quality labels.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: You are talking about clustering, right? In the case of unsupervised learning. Yeah, but it works a bit differently because with unsupervised learning, you can apply something like K-means clustering and cluster your data into three or five clusters, for example. Then you need to analyze these clusters and assign the labels to them. That's how it works. The clustering algorithm by itself doesn't apply labels to the data samples. It just allows you to cluster the data into groups, and then it simplifies further analysis for you. So you can see the common patterns and use these common patterns in the group to label the data. Maybe you know something about transfer learning? You mentioned you had experience with generative AI, and you're probably familiar with the concept of fine-tuning. What does fine-tuning and transfer learning mean? We can use them interchangeably. Could you tell more about it?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So, if I understand it correctly, in some cases, we have already available models that have their structure in the system, and we have options to use that data in our new system. In the case of fine-tuning, we can use that knowledge and do some tuning on that data by adding some new information. So, we can use the data we already have, especially the weight matrix from one model, to create another model without training, but by using that data from the first model.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. To summarize it, we can take a model that was pre-trained for another task and fine-tune it for your specific task. This is often the case in the NLP field when we take an open-source, let's say, BERT model, and then we can fine-tune it for your classification task or for sentiment analysis. If, for example, your texts are domain-specific, the model might not know about these specifics of your domain. You can label your data and then fine-tune an already pre-trained model to solve your task. Actually, I used fine-tuning in educational cases, and one time for a client, we did fine-tuning when we needed a structured output. It was a task or problem where the model didn't give the output in the form we wanted, even with prompt tuning. So, we used fine-tuning and a specific corpus of data for that business, for that customer, to fine-tune that model. And if I understand correctly, you asked about transfer learning. This is the same concept but more general because transfer learning and fine-tuning can be used interchangeably, and it applies not only to transformer models. It can be applied to computer vision tasks when we take something like a ResNet neural network architecture, and then we can fine-tune the dense layer to classify images. So, it doesn't apply only to NLP; it can be applied to other areas of machine learning. At its core, it's a technique where the model was pre-trained for one task on a big corpus of data, and then we can use a much smaller dataset. This is why it resolves the data labeling problem: we don't need a lot of labeled data samples to train our model; we can just fine-tune the existing one.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, I understand. So it is also in the case when we don't have a limited amount of labels, and it is one of the approaches.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, I think we can move to the next question. Let's shortly talk about feature engineering. What feature engineering techniques do you know?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So, when we want to train our model, we need some features. Feature engineering techniques depend on the kind of data. For numerical data, we can use normalization, right? For categorical data, we can use another approach, like one-hot encoding. We need to make these features useful for our system. One option, with timestamp data, is that we can pick up some useful information, which is a kind of one-hot encoding, I think, when we can pick up date, or month, or week—some special information from that. Also, we can create new features from existing data by some manipulation, especially with longitude features. These are like coordinates that we can get, or the opposite, from the location in the world.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: What about handling missing values?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Missing values are actually a problem for machine learning, and we have two main approaches. One of them is just to delete the data that has missing values. It could be deleting a table or deleting rows with missing values. The other approach is imputation of data. So, according to some statistical approaches, it could be filling the data by some average data, like mode or median. Also, interpolation could be used to find...

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. I think it's enough. Could you compare deletion and imputation? What are their pros and cons?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah. So, in the case of deleting information, deleting rows, it's possible that we delete some important features, thereby limiting some information in our database. In the case of imputation, we keep the size of our data the same and just fill in missing values with some temporary or useful, informative values that are statistically correct.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, I think the problem with imputation is that we can add noisy data. We can derive the statistics, but it's not necessarily related to the sample we apply imputation on. This data might not be very correct; it could be noise from that data. It's just an assumption we derive from the statistical distribution, for example, and just replace the missing value with this assumption, for example, with the mean. For instance, with median income, we can derive the average of all incomes available in our dataset, but it's not necessarily true for one particular sample where this value is missing. And if I understand correctly, imputation is a more difficult process than just deletion, right? So we should understand if it works or not for our case. Of course, yeah, and this is why when you have missing values, you need to investigate why they are missing. They can be missing completely at random, and if there are a couple of cases of completely random missing values, then you probably can just delete them. But for example, if there's a correlation, say you have some data samples with medium income, and this medium income is missing, but medium income can be missing because, I don't know, richer people don't want to expose their revenue or show public data. So this is what you should keep in mind. The main knowledge is required for feature engineering, and actually, I would say, the better you handle features and missing values, the more accurate the model could be.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, if I understand, it's really important. Because the quality of your model will really depend on how you prepare your data. Right.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, great. I think let's talk about the training process. What is the bias-variance tradeoff during training?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yep. So, bias and variance are kinds of errors we achieve at the moment of producing our model, especially when our model is trained on training data. If we use a very simple algorithm for complex data, we can achieve bias errors because the data is so complex, and our system can't handle it in the normal case. In the case of variance, it's a question of how well it's adapted after training to real-world data. And variance there, so it's a parameter to understand how well our model is generalized for new data. Maybe it's not very clear.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: No, you are moving in the right direction. But I would say, you said that we can take a simple model, and it might not learn the patterns from complex data. This is the case where you will have high variance in the training because the errors between the actual values and the predicted values will be too big. I agree. So it is high variance. The tradeoff is to find an algorithm or approach to have approximately low variance and bias, but their relationship is opposite. So, when we have low bias, we usually have higher variance, and vice versa. In that case, the task is to find an appropriate algorithm to work with that complexity and produce good predictions. Yeah, it makes sense.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. Another question regarding model training. What do you know about ensemble learning?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Ensemble learning is one approach in machine learning where we use not only one model for our task, but a set of models that produce a better output or better final result than any single model. In that case, what I know are some approaches like boosting, bagging, and probably stacking. Their difference is in how they work with those models. In the case of bagging, it could be one set of data, but separated into different groups, and different models will be trained on that set of data. That data will be exchanged; part of it will be for training, and part for validation. These models will be trained separately, and then for the final result, they will produce an answer together. So, it could be a kind of voting in classification tasks, right, for the biggest votes for that output, or it could be some approximation of the results in regression tasks. In the case of boosting, it's a different approach where models learn one by one. So, one starts and has some limitations or errors, and the second model learns and tries to improve or handle these errors from the first model. And in stacking, I think it's just a lot of models working together to create some grand model, like a master model, by the aggregation of different models at the same time.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. I think it's enough. Let's then move to the deployment part. What do you know about the difference between batch prediction and online prediction? Can you tell more about use cases where batch prediction is more appropriate and where online prediction is more appropriate?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yes. Batch prediction is a kind of prediction that works on a huge amount of data, probably. It doesn't need to be done fast; it's a batch or chunk of tasks that should be done. The timing isn't very important, but it requires a lot of computation for that kind of task. In real-time prediction or stream prediction, it is time-sensitive, so our model can do predictions near real-time. Data just arrives at the model, and the model is ready to produce the answer. In batch, we can schedule this job for later, or it could have some triggers to do that job. I have some practical cases, like when I was working with the task to recognize documents, and it was a batch of documents. It wasn't very important to do it immediately. I just started that job when it was convenient, and especially in clouds, it's possible to use cheap computation to produce these workloads. It's done in batch. When I created a chatbot, and there's a real-time question from the user to the model, it's streaming prediction, where the model answers approximately at near real-time. So, it's for real-time analysis or conversation with the model.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, I think it sounds reasonable. Okay. Also, regarding the model deployment process, in general, we have two options: we can either deploy the model to the cloud or to an edge device, like your smartphone or a desktop application. Can you compare the pros and cons of these approaches? Can you provide some examples of techniques that allow you to reduce the model size?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yes. Cloud deployment is very popular because it offers many opportunities, especially what I like is scaling. When many users approach your endpoint to talk to your model, it can scale in size. Also, many services in the cloud are managed automatically for you, so you don't need to manage that infrastructure. One of the advantages is that some models require a lot of computation, maybe some GPUs for that computation, some storage, some memory, which is good in the cloud for deployment. But there are also some limitations in the cloud because we need an internet connection to connect to our model. One problem I had with my clients about the cloud was data governance; they had restrictions that data should not leave the country area. In the case of edge device deployment, there are also some limitations. Many models are really huge, and it's difficult to use them on edge devices because they also need space, memory, and some additional resources for processing. On the other hand, there are some techniques that can make models much smaller to use them locally. Yes, I think one of the most popular techniques is quantization, where we decrease the precision of weights. According to that, we have, for example, 8-bit instead of 32-bit decimals, making it much smaller. So, it's a kind of quantization when we decrease that size. I also know about knowledge distillation. I think it's like transfer knowledge, which we were talking about, transferring knowledge and distillation knowledge, where from a huge model, we distill its weights, but not all of them are important to continue that kind of task. Pruning is also a possibility to decrease the size of the model, but I think quantization might be the main one in that case. In edge devices, there's also a plus, opposite to the cloud, that we can work with our private data locally without the internet with our model. If there are some restrictions for data usage, edge device deployment could be more suitable for those kinds of tasks.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. Yeah, sounds reasonable. Thank you. Okay. Let's assume we've deployed our model to a production environment. But the machine learning lifecycle never ends, and you need to monitor model performance because there are different reasons why the performance of the model can decrease, and one of them is data distribution shift. Could you please tell more about it? What does it mean, and what techniques do you know for detecting data distribution shifts?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, about data distribution shifts. It's a problem we can face when we've trained our model on some data, and in a real-world scenario, when our model is already in production, there could be some changes in the data's nature. The situation changed, and the real data and data from training have different quality. For example, if we trained our model on some historical data, and now we're going to the market, and there's a new contributor or company providing a new product, the behavior of customers has changed. In that case, we have this data drift. To understand it, we need to monitor the performance of our model to see if it's degrading. There could also be feedback from users, like some up or down votes. To understand this data drift, monitoring and logging are one way. I know there are some statistical approaches to understand if data has similar features or not. I didn't work with them directly, but I remember some statistical methods, like a statistical approach to understand the difference or similarity in the data. What else could be used there? I think other models, other kinds of artificial intelligence, could also be used to understand the drift, to see the difference in the data, like anomaly detections.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. We don't have a lot of time to discuss it, so I'll ask a couple of questions about MLOps, and then we'll move to the coding task. My first question would be: let's assume you have a team of machine learning engineers and data scientists, and to make their work efficient enough, you need to standardize the development environment so that it's reproducible, and you don't encounter situations where code works on one machine but not on another. What would you do to standardize the development environment and streamline this development workflow?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, I think it's very similar to DevOps, this paradigm of microservices where we can use container environments. Also, additional approaches for standardization, like virtual environments for our dependencies or, in Python, a `requirements.txt` file to collect all dependencies to keep them consistent. Especially with containerization, we can achieve the same environment on any device, in any situation. So, we just set the operating system, if we need it, or dependencies and libraries—everything we need there, we can have it standardized in one container. We can call it a container, right? And next, it gives us the possibility to scale or reproduce it on any system because it's a closed environment inside this container.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, I think what you said about containers, containerization is beneficial, of course, especially for deployment and auto-scaling. But usually, let's assume you're just a developer; you open an IDE and write code. You don't usually make adjustments in your code in the Docker container. So, the Docker container is usually the last phase when you package your code into the container, and this container can be run on a different machine. But during development, you mentioned that you can log your requirements. What's important, and also maybe version control systems, is useful for that, right? Like when we can control our code, keep it steady, and collaborate with the team. And also, it's a kind of automation there. And I also wanted to mention locking requirements. It's very important because if you just list the names of different packages and libraries without the exact version, it might be the case that whenever a new team member joins your team and pulls the repository into their local machine, they can encounter a situation where the environment is not reproducible because when they install the dependencies, they can install different versions from what you have on your machine. I understand, and it can actually break the whole process; the application might not work.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Actually, I saw it on some... I don't remember. It's a kind of different approach to store and log our dependencies. I don't remember how it goes, but in my case, I mostly use `requirements.txt` with versions. I don't know if it's an appropriate approach in that case.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, I'd say it's completely workable. You can just run `pip freeze` command to lock your dependencies. But there are different tools. I'm a fan of UV, the latest development for the Python ecosystem. It actually replaces all the tools for the Python ecosystem and combines them into one tool, and you can easily lock your dependencies, manage your package, manage your project. So, this is a very good tool for standardization as well. And another way, I would say, you can go further. You can actually use a cloud environment for development and force the team to use the cloud environment. And it allows the system administration team not to manage many devices and control what's installed there and so on. So, you just have one standardized environment in the cloud. But of course, there's a con: the cloud requires spending money.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Oh, yeah.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: And I think we can move on to some generative AI questions. Let's talk shortly about prompt engineering. What prompt engineering techniques do you know to get better results from language models?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: There are some approaches and recommendations. What I know, recommendations are maybe more useful for a wide spread of people who use generative AI applications. So, it's a kind of recommendation: when you talk to your data, you should be specific, objective. You can give a role to the model to shorten or narrow the field where the model will look for information. It's also possible to provide context. Context is one of the important things, I think, for instructions, and it could also be an instruction for the output type you want to receive. You can also give some examples as context. In that case, with examples, it's already a kind of technique for prompt engineering; it's called one-shot prompting or multi-shot prompting when you give examples to the model. Zero-shot prompting is just a prompt without examples. It's possible now to talk to the model and receive the chain of thought of that model, and this technique is called Chain-of-Thought. It's possible to achieve this by adding some data to the prompt where you ask it to 'think step by step' or 'provide the reasoning of your answers.' There are also some modern approaches, additional ones like Chain-of-Thought. So, these are really techniques on how you can improve, and there's a really big difference in the outputs depending on your approach to talk to the model. Actually, RAG is also a kind of prompt engineering used to achieve good results from the model.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: I wouldn't say it's a prompt engineering technique. It's actually the pattern of how a generative application might be built.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I think maybe, yeah, probably you are correct. I mean that when we are picking up context and giving it to the model, RAG gives us the possibility to create additional content to give to the model. But yeah, probably a framework.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: No, no, I think it's an acceptable answer. Okay, can you describe in detail how the RAG system might look like?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So, when we have base models or general models like GPT, Gemini, or Claude, they have limitations. They only have the knowledge they were trained on or what they are given by context. So, they can have some limitations. When we talk about business usage, I think it's one of the main directions for businesses to adopt generative artificial intelligence to their knowledge base. All of them have information, and RAG gives the possibility to use this knowledge base with generative AI. How does it work? It's a combination of retrieval and generation. When we have our documents, for example, a PDF document, we send it to the system. The system chunks it into the smallest parts. Those smallest parts are then decoded into vectors. It's not just text; in that case, text becomes numbers, like floating numbers, a vector of floating numbers, which are embeddings. The machine can already read and understand those embeddings. So, those embeddings are our knowledge, and from them, we build our knowledge base. These embeddings will be stored in a vector database, which can store them and separate them by their fields or space in the database. It gives the possibility, according to a new incoming embedding, to find similar embeddings, like some neighbors of that embedding. It's called similarity search, right? And by semantic search, it gives us the possibility to find information according to the semantic information of that part of the text, if it is text. So, in our case, when the user asks a question, this question is also converted to an embedding, using the same system as before when we converted our text. According to that embedding, in our vector database, we look for similar documents from the base. Next, we give the LLM our original question from the user and those parts of documents that were retrieved additionally, and we provide them as context. There are also some additional options, maybe to fine-tune, like we can re-rank or check the most appropriate parts we picked up from the database to provide to the LLM, not all the information, but maybe just the most relevant information we need. Also, we have limitations of LLMs, especially with the context window, the amount of information we can give to the LLM. In the case of RAG, we are handling that problem by just putting all the necessary information to provide a good answer.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, but you've mostly described the case of using vector databases and semantic search. Is RAG limited to semantic search, or can we use something else? Can we just translate the text into SQL and make a search in this classical relational database in order to enrich our prompt with structured data?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yes, sorry. So it's another approach, and in that case, we need some additional data, not data from the database, but we need our data to create these queries, but it's also possible. So, we give information about our table to our LLM. The LLM can produce an SQL query according to that schema. Next, it could be executed in the database, or actually, it could also be in memory if it's not a huge amount. According to the SQL result, we provide the LLM with the query and the result to get the final result. It could actually be tables or also a graphical review or graphical view, which could also be useful.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Actually, to summarize RAG, we can say that the main part is that we can enrich our prompt with external information and ask the model to generate the answer. But it might be structured data from a relational database, it might be semantic search when we search for similar text in a vector store, or it might even be searching the internet. For example, if we ask a question about the latest financial news or sports news, the LLM has a limitation because it might be trained on data only up to, say, 2024. Now we want to get news information from the internet. So, actually, it's called an agentic RAG when we have multiple choices where we can retrieve information to enrich our prompt. In any case, it's like retrieval of additional information. It's a very generic thing; it's not only related to vector search.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I agree. Understood. Didn't think about it.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: But yeah, got it. Okay. And probably the last question for this part: when is it beneficial to fine-tune the language model?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I understood. From my experience, what I remember from the education part, when we can't achieve the result we need from our LLM, especially when we have outputs we didn't expect, and we can't achieve it even with prompt engineering or RAG, we can use fine-tuning to achieve that task. It could be a specific type of answer if we are trying to achieve a short answer and especially structure it in some way. If we can't achieve it from prompt engineering, we can use fine-tuning for that. Or also, if we have some specific data and specific terminology that is unique to our field, we can use that data to fine-tune our model to increase its knowledge about those terms. Or maybe if there's some specificity to your business, and you have examples of how questions should be answered, like a golden standard, it's also possible to fine-tune the model like for that reason, to achieve the results you are looking for.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, yeah, that makes sense. Okay, I think we are ready to move to the final part of our interview: the practical task. You have a list of dictionaries. If you scroll down, you can see the example input. So, you have a list of dictionaries, and each dictionary can contain two keys, but it can be any number of keys. One of these keys is 'flex name' and 'flex', right? Your task is to write a function that extracts all unique flags across all entries. Then you add a new key to each dictionary for every flag, and the value should be one if the user has this flag and zero if the user doesn't have this flag. So, you do something like one-hot encoding in this example. Then you need to remove the 'flags' key and replace it with one-hot encoding. You can see the expected output. Your function has to return the updated list of dictionaries with one-hot encoding and the list of all unique flags that are available in this list. So here they are like strings, right? And we will provide them as key and value. And these flags must be sorted alphabetically. I think maybe you can just explain with your voice what you're going to do next.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yes, I'm looking now for input and output. So, we have a list of dictionaries, right? And next, we will have a set of lists. Okay, about sorted. So, it should probably not be a list; it should be a set in our case, `flags_set`, to have them unique. I can sort it later. First, we should pick up our line and check it for `entry in data`. If `flags` is there in `entry`, and we have this flag, so it is not empty, `flags_from_entry`. It could be possible, but it is `flags`, it is a line. So, we have them unique, and we need just sorted, right? Sorry, a moment, just checking if I'm correct here or not: 'premium test user'. Okay. With that part, it looks like it is working. Now we need a new list. Now we are iterating through the lines to pick up to create a new dictionary, right? We need the first key and additional keys. So, the first key, `new_line` is...

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, right. So, for the moment, we have the list of flags, and we are creating a new list where there will be dictionaries: a dictionary with a 'name' parameter and a dictionary with keys of the flag entities. Now, I need to pick up this string of flags from the original data and, according to that, pick up each of them. For available flags, I want to map them as one, and if they are not inside my parameters, I will mark them as zero. Right? And next, it will be added as keys and values to our list of dictionaries, and it should look like what you asked about, if I'm correct. So, we will have a list where every line is a dictionary with a 'name' key, and different flags will have different keys, and their values will be zero or one.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, I may say that the flow in general is correct. There are a couple of edge cases that we may cover here in the function, but we don't have enough time to discuss it. But in general, I think I understand what you are going to do.

turns.speaker: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Guys, please don't finish this up because I have more questions for you, not part of the interview. It's not going to happen in the real world, or it's going to happen in a week or so, but now we are on the show, so we have the opportunity to have small feedback on both sides, which I think will be really useful. First of all, I would like to ask how it went, how did you like the structure, how did you feel, what was the most complex question, what answers did you like, and how was Dennis for you as the person who was asking? I saw that Dennis was helping a lot, and how did it feel, and how did it go for you?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Thank you for that experience. Actually, it is interesting, it is cool. The field covers a lot of information, and to be 100% prepared is not simple, especially it's simpler when you have some practical knowledge on some of the questions or parts. Of course, if it's just raw information in your brain from education, it's not simple to understand. But especially questions about RAG or some generative AI capabilities are more interesting or maybe simpler for me because most of the time I'm working with that part. So, I'm happy. Also, on the final task, I maybe needed some time to understand and think how to do it the correct way.

turns.speaker: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Let's not forget that you knew in your mind that you were recorded and going to be on YouTube, so I think this is something that can actually put some pressure on you. So, I think you were doing great. I think it really... Thank you. I hope my mind was actually blowing during your session. Okay. Thank you so much. Thank you, Dennis. Thank you, Alex.

turns.speaker: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Thank you. What do you think? I would like to ask you two things. First of all, the official feedback: how you would actually do it in the real world. This is one part. And the other part would be really nice if you would give feedback as if you were Mahilo's friend, because I think it's really useful for people to hear both sides of that. Okay.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So, the official one: of course, there are some gaps in knowledge because the machine learning field is very huge, and you need to educate yourself a lot. But I think, at the same time, in many cases, Mahilo clearly shared his thoughts and the core ideas, so it's a big advantage. He was also on the right track in the coding task. I would say Mahilo is not ready for a senior position, but for a middle one, he is ready to participate in projects and complete tasks. I would recommend structuring the knowledge in different stages of the ML lifecycle, like data preparation, data labeling, and feature engineering. These are very important concepts because ML is not limited only to generative AI. There is computer vision, working with structured data, because sometimes it's better to just use a simple algorithm that everybody understands, and it's easy to interpret the results of the model. Actually, it might be less heavy-weighted for inference in production. So, it's not necessary to deploy a very complex neural network. It might be costly for inference, and it might be less interpretive. So, my recommendation is to strengthen the knowledge in different areas of machine learning conceptual knowledge. But I think it's an evolutionary process in education, so I think you are on the right path to becoming a good professional. This is how I would provide the official feedback. And just as a friend, to be honest, there's no big difference because I always try to provide direct feedback and highlight areas to improve. But my personal recommendation is to try not to focus only on the fancy stuff but also on the very fundamental stuff like data preparation and data labeling, because these are the dirty parts of the process, but they are very important. You know, there are a lot of algorithms that you can apply to your data, but if you feed bad data into this algorithm, your solution will not work. And also the deployment part. When you develop your model, it's not the end of the lifecycle. You need to deploy, you need to maintain, and you need to establish good MLOps best practices to streamline the ML development and deployment process. So, just my personal recommendation: focus on less fancy things because they are fundamental ones, and this is what will differentiate you from others who are in the race for fancy stuff like generative LLMs. Sometimes even a simple rule-based system can work better than an ML one.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, I wrote down some of the points from your recommendation. Thanks, Dennis. We'll follow and know where to move.

turns.speaker: UNKNOWN
turns.text: So there we have it. It's another mock interview series completed. If you enjoyed this video and found it helpful, don't forget to check out the links in the description to learn more about Njax and how it's transforming the way we work. Also, make sure to subscribe to our YouTube channel and share this video with anyone who is going to have something like that in the future or just wants to check out how it can be in the real world. This was Alexi Molashina, this was Dennis with us, and Mahilo. Thank you guys, and thanks for watching. See you next time.

training_pairs.question: As I said, let's start with the first section. Could you tell more about your experience, what projects you worked on, and what your achievements are?
training_pairs.answer: Okay, Dennis. Thank you. Nice to meet you. About me: most of the time I'm studying, but during those studying steps, especially like certification preparations, I gain some practice step by step. Especially in the last two years, I worked mostly with GCP Cloud, specifically with generative AI capabilities. I was working in a company that is an integrator on the market, working with different vendors. I was a pre-sale engineer for GCP Cloud, focusing on generative AI. My tasks were mostly to talk to clients to understand what they wanted to achieve with this new technology. Most of the time, I worked on PoCs, proof-of-concept for business ideas, to understand if they were possible to implement. It wasn't a lot of production coding, but for the last half-year, I've been working on an internal product, also with generative artificial intelligence. My task is to adopt, create, and adapt an SQL agent, which is a different approach to picking up knowledge. Most generative applications with private domain data work by semantic search. For me, the approach with structured data is interesting: how we can work with it, especially how we can combine it with semantic search. So, at the moment, I'm approaching the agentic flow in generative applications to combine these two great capabilities. I'm also exploring a SQL generation agent that can talk to databases or local files with structured data. So, that is my main task and main field of work at the moment.
training_pairs.follow_up: Okay, got it. I wanted to ask about your experience with GCP. What services did you work with? What did they actually do for your clients and for your solutions?

training_pairs.question: Okay, got it. I wanted to ask about your experience with GCP. What services did you work with? What did they actually do for your clients and for your solutions?
training_pairs.answer: Okay, actually, there's an interesting tool called Agent Builder, which helps build agentic applications out-of-the-box with great capabilities. Before that, to understand how it works, I was learning Dialogflow, which is also a framework to build connections between a person's intent and the program's fallback. So, understanding this routine and intents was my way to understand how everything works in NLP from that side. But also, from this out-of-the-box tool, I started to use code to customize tasks, and with code, I became familiar with LangChain, which is a great framework to build these applications. Regarding the tasks I've done, one was searching by image in an image database. This was an interesting business task from a client: if someone took a photo of something, was it possible to find this item in the store? I used embeddings. Specifically, I used different costs. First, I created descriptions of the pictures, then stored the descriptions in a vector database. Next, a new incoming image was again decoded into a natural description, and according to semantic search, it picked up similar images from the vector database. I also built a chatbot for one of our customers and connected it to Telegram. That was a new experience for me, using a new kind of UI to talk to an agent or chatbot. Interesting tasks also included one client who had their call center calls recorded. My task was to extract text from those audio calls to understand if the sentiment was positive, negative, or neutral. Another interesting task was for a customer who had many typical old documents. Their task was to recognize entities on those documents. So, I recognized the documents, and as output, the LLM gave me a JSON structured answer with all the entities I needed from those documents. With the help of LLM, it was really possible to achieve good results, especially with large amounts of data. So, it was different tasks in different fields.
training_pairs.follow_up: Okay, so as I understood, most of your experience is about generative AI. You didn't work with traditional ML, right? For example, computer vision or working with structured data?

training_pairs.question: What sampling methods to get training data do you know?
training_pairs.answer: Okay, I understand. So there is sampling from the data. I know one approach, maybe the simplest one, is randomized sampling, where all of our data has an equal opportunity to be picked up for our sample. It's a statistical method. Also, there could be options like a kind of random but more systematic sampling, where we have some groups and pick up an element from that group, and then with some step, we pick up elements from those subgroups. It's important to know that we have all important data for our sample, and in that case, stratified sampling is common when we have subgroups, especially when some subgroups are represented with a low amount of examples. It could be an option to pick up all important information from there. Also, there could be convenient sampling, just picking up all available data that we can use at the moment without any specific approach.
training_pairs.follow_up: What is the main drawback of this approach? If you sample the data just by convenience, what is the main drawback?

training_pairs.question: Do you know the difference between hand labels and natural labels?
training_pairs.answer: Yes. Hand labels, I think, are probably the main approach, especially historically, when a human takes an example and assigns a label to that example. In natural labels, it's a different approach where we have some data that is like an added bonus to our data; it already has some information. For example, it could be a timestamp in the data, or it could be part of the file name that could be used by the model. Or, in that kind of labeling, it could be used to assign some simple, maybe pre-labeled information, like to pick up some information to categorize it for the moment. So, it's some additional information that comes to us with our examples, correct?
training_pairs.follow_up: I would say you've clearly described hand labeling. It's obvious that people label the data manually. But regarding natural labels, I think you were going in the wrong direction. I'll provide an example. Let's assume there's a recommendation system that recommends different movies or products that users will mostly like. We can measure the effectiveness of the recommendation system by, for example, whether the user clicks on this product or movie or not. If the user clicks on this product, we log this information, and we obtain the natural label that this recommendation is good enough to be clicked. Then we can reuse this data for model training. So, we receive feedback from the system on whether users click on these products or not, and then we can reuse this data for retraining. This is the nature of natural labels: they are derived automatically by the system.

training_pairs.question: What does fine-tuning and transfer learning mean? We can use them interchangeably. Could you tell more about it?
training_pairs.answer: So, if I understand it correctly, in some cases, we have already available models that have their structure in the system, and we have options to use that data in our new system. In the case of fine-tuning, we can use that knowledge and do some tuning on that data by adding some new information. So, we can use the data we already have, especially the weight matrix from one model, to create another model without training, but by using that data from the first model.
training_pairs.follow_up: Okay. To summarize it, we can take a model that was pre-trained for another task and fine-tune it for your specific task. This is often the case in the NLP field when we take an open-source, let's say, BERT model, and then we can fine-tune it for your classification task or for sentiment analysis. If, for example, your texts are domain-specific, the model might not know about these specifics of your domain. You can label your data and then fine-tune an already pre-trained model to solve your task. Actually, I used fine-tuning in educational cases, and one time for a client, we did fine-tuning when we needed a structured output. It was a task or problem where the model didn't give the output in the form we wanted, even with prompt tuning. So, we used fine-tuning and a specific corpus of data for that business, for that customer, to fine-tune that model. And if I understand correctly, you asked about transfer learning. This is the same concept but more general because transfer learning and fine-tuning can be used interchangeably, and it applies not only to transformer models. It can be applied to computer vision tasks when we take something like a ResNet neural network architecture, and then we can fine-tune the dense layer to classify images. So, it doesn't apply only to NLP; it can be applied to other areas of machine learning. At its core, it's a technique where the model was pre-trained for one task on a big corpus of data, and then we can use a much smaller dataset. This is why it resolves the data labeling problem: we don't need a lot of labeled data samples to train our model; we can just fine-tune the existing one.

training_pairs.question: What feature engineering techniques do you know? What about handling missing values? Could you compare deletion and imputation? What are their pros and cons?
training_pairs.answer: So, when we want to train our model, we need some features. Feature engineering techniques depend on the kind of data. For numerical data, we can use normalization, right? For categorical data, we can use another approach, like one-hot encoding. We need to make these features useful for our system. One option, with timestamp data, is that we can pick up some useful information, which is a kind of one-hot encoding, I think, when we can pick up date, or month, or week—some special information from that. Also, we can create new features from existing data by some manipulation, especially with longitude features. These are like coordinates that we can get, or the opposite, from the location in the world. Missing values are actually a problem for machine learning, and we have two main approaches. One of them is just to delete the data that has missing values. It could be deleting a table or deleting rows with missing values. The other approach is imputation of data. So, according to some statistical approaches, it could be filling the data by some average data, like mode or median. Also, interpolation could be used to find... In the case of deleting information, deleting rows, it's possible that we delete some important features, thereby limiting some information in our database. In the case of imputation, we keep the size of our data the same and just fill in missing values with some temporary or useful, informative values that are statistically correct.
training_pairs.follow_up: Yeah, I think the problem with imputation is that we can add noisy data. We can derive the statistics, but it's not necessarily related to the sample we apply imputation on. This data might not be very correct; it could be noise from that data. It's just an assumption we derive from the statistical distribution, for example, and just replace the missing value with this assumption, for example, with the mean. For instance, with median income, we can derive the average of all incomes available in our dataset, but it's not necessarily true for one particular sample where this value is missing. And if I understand correctly, imputation is a more difficult process than just deletion, right? So we should understand if it works or not for our case. Of course, yeah, and this is why when you have missing values, you need to investigate why they are missing. They can be missing completely at random, and if there are a couple of cases of completely random missing values, then you probably can just delete them. But for example, if there's a correlation, say you have some data samples with medium income, and this medium income is missing, but medium income can be missing because, I don't know, richer people don't want to expose their revenue or show public data. So this is what you should keep in mind. The main knowledge is required for feature engineering, and actually, I would say, the better you handle features and missing values, the more accurate the model could be.

training_pairs.question: What is the bias-variance tradeoff during training?
training_pairs.answer: Yep. So, bias and variance are kinds of errors we achieve at the moment of producing our model, especially when our model is trained on training data. If we use a very simple algorithm for complex data, we can achieve bias errors because the data is so complex, and our system can't handle it in the normal case. In the case of variance, it's a question of how well it's adapted after training to real-world data. And variance there, so it's a parameter to understand how well our model is generalized for new data. Maybe it's not very clear.
training_pairs.follow_up: No, you are moving in the right direction. But I would say, you said that we can take a simple model, and it might not learn the patterns from complex data. This is the case where you will have high variance in the training because the errors between the actual values and the predicted values will be too big. I agree. So it is high variance. The tradeoff is to find an algorithm or approach to have approximately low variance and bias, but their relationship is opposite. So, when we have low bias, we usually have higher variance, and vice versa. In that case, the task is to find an appropriate algorithm to work with that complexity and produce good predictions. Yeah, it makes sense.

training_pairs.question: What do you know about ensemble learning?
training_pairs.answer: Ensemble learning is one approach in machine learning where we use not only one model for our task, but a set of models that produce a better output or better final result than any single model. In that case, what I know are some approaches like boosting, bagging, and probably stacking. Their difference is in how they work with those models. In the case of bagging, it could be one set of data, but separated into different groups, and different models will be trained on that set of data. That data will be exchanged; part of it will be for training, and part for validation. These models will be trained separately, and then for the final result, they will produce an answer together. So, it could be a kind of voting in classification tasks, right, for the biggest votes for that output, or it could be some approximation of the results in regression tasks. In the case of boosting, it's a different approach where models learn one by one. So, one starts and has some limitations or errors, and the second model learns and tries to improve or handle these errors from the first model. And in stacking, I think it's just a lot of models working together to create some grand model, like a master model, by the aggregation of different models at the same time.

training_pairs.question: What do you know about the difference between batch prediction and online prediction? Can you tell more about use cases where batch prediction is more appropriate and where online prediction is more appropriate?
training_pairs.answer: Yes. Batch prediction is a kind of prediction that works on a huge amount of data, probably. It doesn't need to be done fast; it's a batch or chunk of tasks that should be done. The timing isn't very important, but it requires a lot of computation for that kind of task. In real-time prediction or stream prediction, it is time-sensitive, so our model can do predictions near real-time. Data just arrives at the model, and the model is ready to produce the answer. In batch, we can schedule this job for later, or it could have some triggers to do that job. I have some practical cases, like when I was working with the task to recognize documents, and it was a batch of documents. It wasn't very important to do it immediately. I just started that job when it was convenient, and especially in clouds, it's possible to use cheap computation to produce these workloads. It's done in batch. When I created a chatbot, and there's a real-time question from the user to the model, it's streaming prediction, where the model answers approximately at near real-time. So, it's for real-time analysis or conversation with the model.

training_pairs.question: In general, we have two options: we can either deploy the model to the cloud or to an edge device, like your smartphone or a desktop application. Can you compare the pros and cons of these approaches? Can you provide some examples of techniques that allow you to reduce the model size?
training_pairs.answer: Yes. Cloud deployment is very popular because it offers many opportunities, especially what I like is scaling. When many users approach your endpoint to talk to your model, it can scale in size. Also, many services in the cloud are managed automatically for you, so you don't need to manage that infrastructure. One of the advantages is that some models require a lot of computation, maybe some GPUs for that computation, some storage, some memory, which is good in the cloud for deployment. But there are also some limitations in the cloud because we need an internet connection to connect to our model. One problem I had with my clients about the cloud was data governance; they had restrictions that data should not leave the country area. In the case of edge device deployment, there are also some limitations. Many models are really huge, and it's difficult to use them on edge devices because they also need space, memory, and some additional resources for processing. On the other hand, there are some techniques that can make models much smaller to use them locally. Yes, I think one of the most popular techniques is quantization, where we decrease the precision of weights. According to that, we have, for example, 8-bit instead of 32-bit decimals, making it much smaller. So, it's a kind of quantization when we decrease that size. I also know about knowledge distillation. I think it's like transfer knowledge, which we were talking about, transferring knowledge and distillation knowledge, where from a huge model, we distill its weights, but not all of them are important to continue that kind of task. Pruning is also a possibility to decrease the size of the model, but I think quantization might be the main one in that case. In edge devices, there's also a plus, opposite to the cloud, that we can work with our private data locally without the internet with our model. If there are some restrictions for data usage, edge device deployment could be more suitable for those kinds of tasks.

training_pairs.question: Can you describe in detail how the RAG system might look like? Is RAG limited to semantic search, or can we use something else? Can we just translate the text into SQL and make a search in this classical relational database in order to enrich our prompt with structured data?
training_pairs.answer: So, when we have base models or general models like GPT, Gemini, or Claude, they have limitations. They only have the knowledge they were trained on or what they are given by context. So, they can have some limitations. When we talk about business usage, I think it's one of the main directions for businesses to adopt generative artificial intelligence to their knowledge base. All of them have information, and RAG gives the possibility to use this knowledge base with generative AI. How does it work? It's a combination of retrieval and generation. When we have our documents, for example, a PDF document, we send it to the system. The system chunks it into the smallest parts. Those smallest parts are then decoded into vectors. It's not just text; in that case, text becomes numbers, like floating numbers, a vector of floating numbers, which are embeddings. The machine can already read and understand those embeddings. So, those embeddings are our knowledge, and from them, we build our knowledge base. These embeddings will be stored in a vector database, which can store them and separate them by their fields or space in the database. It gives the possibility, according to a new incoming embedding, to find similar embeddings, like some neighbors of that embedding. It's called similarity search, right? And by semantic search, it gives us the possibility to find information according to the semantic information of that part of the text, if it is text. So, in our case, when the user asks a question, this question is also converted to an embedding, using the same system as before when we converted our text. According to that embedding, in our vector database, we look for similar documents from the base. Next, we give the LLM our original question from the user and those parts of documents that were retrieved additionally, and we provide them as context. There are also some additional options, maybe to fine-tune, like we can re-rank or check the most appropriate parts we picked up from the database to provide to the LLM, not all the information, but maybe just the most relevant information we need. Also, we have limitations of LLMs, especially with the context window, the amount of information we can give to the LLM. In the case of RAG, we are handling that problem by just putting all the necessary information to provide a good answer. Yes, sorry. So it's another approach, and in that case, we need some additional data, not data from the database, but we need our data to create these queries, but it's also possible. So, we give information about our table to our LLM. The LLM can produce an SQL query according to that schema. Next, it could be executed in the database, or actually, it could also be in memory if it's not a huge amount. According to the SQL result, we provide the LLM with the query and the result to get the final result. It could actually be tables or also a graphical review or graphical view, which could also be useful.
training_pairs.follow_up: Actually, to summarize RAG, we can say that the main part is that we can enrich our prompt with external information and ask the model to generate the answer. But it might be structured data from a relational database, it might be semantic search when we search for similar text in a vector store, or it might even be searching the internet. For example, if we ask a question about the latest financial news or sports news, the LLM has a limitation because it might be trained on data only up to, say, 2024. Now we want to get news information from the internet. So, actually, it's called an agentic RAG when we have multiple choices where we can retrieve information to enrich our prompt. In any case, it's like retrieval of additional information. It's a very generic thing; it's not only related to vector search.

training_pairs.question: When is it beneficial to fine-tune the language model?
training_pairs.answer: I understood. From my experience, what I remember from the education part, when we can't achieve the result we need from our LLM, especially when we have outputs we didn't expect, and we can't achieve it even with prompt engineering or RAG, we can use fine-tuning to achieve that task. It could be a specific type of answer if we are trying to achieve a short answer and especially structure it in some way. If we can't achieve it from prompt engineering, we can use fine-tuning for that. Or also, if we have some specific data and specific terminology that is unique to our field, we can use that data to fine-tune our model to increase its knowledge about those terms. Or maybe if there's some specificity to your business, and you have some examples of how questions should be answered, like a golden standard, it's also possible to fine-tune the model like for that reason, to achieve the results you are looking for.
